meta.title: Rate Limiting: From Bypassable to Production-Grade
meta.excerpt: How a code review uncovered a critical security flaw where rate limiting could be bypassed

props.text: During Code Review #2 (Security Review), we discovered that the service-request form's rate limiting was implemented using an in-memory JavaScript Map. This meant every server restart, deployment, or serverless cold start reset the counter to zero—effectively making the rate limiter bypassable.

items.title: Deployment Resets Counter
items.description: Every Vercel deployment created fresh process, resetting rateLimitMap to empty

items.title: Serverless Cold Starts
items.description: Serverless functions spin up fresh instances. Each cold start = fresh Map = no rate limit history

items.title: No CSRF Protection
items.description: Cross-origin requests could submit forms without validation

items.title: No Bot Detection
items.description: Automated scripts could submit hundreds of requests

items.title: IP-Only Fingerprint
items.description: Using only IP address. Attackers behind NAT could bypass by sharing IP

items.title: No Admin Alerts
items.description: When rate limits hit, no one knew. Attacks went unnoticed

props.title: Real Attack Scenario
props.body: Attacker submits 5 spam requests (hits rate limit). Waits 10 minutes for scheduled deployment or triggers cold start. Rate limit Map resets to empty. Submits 5 more spam requests. Repeat indefinitely. Estimated spam throughput: 40 submissions per day with zero cost to attacker.

props.text: We implemented a three-layer defence-in-depth strategy: persistent rate limiting using an external store (surviving restarts), CSRF token validation on all form submissions, and honeypot fields for bot detection. Each layer operates independently.

steps.title: Layer 3: Honeypot (First Check - Cheapest)
steps.description: Hidden field that bots fill but humans don't see. Instant rejection with zero cost

steps.title: Layer 2: CSRF Validation
steps.description: Double-submit cookie pattern validates request came from legitimate form

steps.title: Layer 1: Persistent Rate Limiting
steps.description: External store tracks requests across deployments and cold starts

steps.title: All Layers Pass → Process Request
steps.description: Only legitimate requests from real users on our site within rate limits reach the database

props.text: The implementation orders checks from cheapest to most expensive: honeypot (nanoseconds), CSRF (microseconds), rate limit (milliseconds). This prevents expensive store queries for obvious bots, improving performance and reducing costs.

props.title: Fingerprint Generation
props.body: Combine multiple signals to create a more robust identifier: IP address (X-Forwarded-For header), User-Agent string, Accept-Language header. Hash these together: hash(ip + userAgent + language). More resistant to spoofing than IP alone, while remaining stateless.

props.title: Why Check Order Matters
props.body: Order checks from cheapest to most expensive: Honeypot: String comparison (nanoseconds). CSRF: Cookie + string comparison (microseconds). Rate limit: External store query (milliseconds). This prevents expensive store queries for obvious bots.

props.text: In-memory rate limiting in serverless or frequently-deployed environments is security theatre—it gives the appearance of protection while providing none. Defence in depth means each layer works independently: if the rate limiter fails, CSRF still blocks cross-origin attacks; if CSRF is bypassed, the honeypot still catches bots. Order your checks from cheapest to most expensive. Never trust a single layer of defence.
